critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design. The temporal split is correctly implemented with a buffer, preprocessing is fit only on training data, and the scope is honestly bounded. I looked hard for fundamental flaws and found only one genuine methodological issue and one design choice worth flagging — but neither is fatal, and one requires empirical resolution.

**Issue 1: The baseline is not a fair comparison for the hypothesis being tested.**

The claim being made is that sequence-derived features improve over static RFM features when holding model class constant, OR that the full system (GBDT + sequence features) beats the operational baseline (LR + RFM). But the experiment conflates two things: model class (logistic regression vs. GBDT) and feature set (RFM vs. RFM + sequence features). If the GBDT + sequence model wins, the gain could be entirely attributable to the model class, entirely attributable to the features, or some combination. The design does not isolate these.

The mechanism of failure: if GBDT with only RFM features outperforms LR with only RFM features by a margin comparable to the observed gain from adding sequence features, then the experiment establishes that GBDT is better than LR, not that sequence features matter. Conversely, if LR with all features matches GBDT with all features, the model class choice is irrelevant and the features are doing all the work.

What would constitute evidence: add two ablation conditions — (a) GBDT trained on RFM-only features, and (b) logistic regression trained on all features. If GBDT+all > GBDT+RFM > LR+RFM, the feature hypothesis is confirmed and model class gives an additive boost. If GBDT+RFM ≈ LR+RFM and GBDT+all >> GBDT+RFM, the features are doing the work. Without these conditions, the headline result is uninterpretable with respect to the stated hypothesis.

**Issue 2: The cohort-churn-propensity feature introduces a subtle form of target leakage.**

The feature described as 'cohort churn propensity' is a group-level statistic derived from historical cancellation behavior of customers in the same cohort. If this statistic is computed using data from customers whose observation dates overlap with or postdate the current customer's observation date within the training window, it encodes future churn rates into the feature. Even within a strict chronological split between train/val/test, intra-training-set leakage is possible if the cohort statistic is computed over the full training period rather than using only data available at each individual observation date.

The mechanism: a customer with an observation date in month 6 would ideally see a cohort propensity computed from months 1–6 only. If the statistic is instead computed from months 1–12 (the full training set), the month-6 customer's feature incorporates churn behavior that happened after their observation date, which is information unavailable at deployment time. This inflates the feature's predictive signal in training without corresponding signal at inference.

What would constitute evidence: inspect whether cohort churn propensity is computed as a point-in-time value (cohort churn rate as of each observation date) or as a static value computed over the entire training window. If it is the latter, re-run with point-in-time computation and check whether validation AUC drops. The text says 'all features are computed as of the observation date using only data available up to and including that date,' which if strictly enforced would prevent this leakage — but the cohort propensity feature is a group-level aggregate that requires careful implementation to satisfy this constraint, and silent misconfiguration here is plausible.

**Summary:**

Issue 1 is a genuine flaw in experimental design — the comparison does not isolate the contribution of features from the contribution of model class, which means the stated hypothesis cannot be confirmed by the proposed experiment as designed. Issue 2 is a plausible but implementation-dependent risk; if the point-in-time constraint is correctly enforced, it is not a flaw.

Issue 1 is structural and cannot be resolved empirically without adding the ablation conditions. It is not a matter of degree — the experiment as described cannot answer its own research question.
all_issues_raised: The baseline conflates model class (LR vs. GBDT) with feature set (RFM vs. sequence features), making it impossible to attribute any observed gain to sequence features specifically without ablation conditions holding one factor constant.

The cohort-churn-propensity feature is a group-level aggregate that risks intra-training-set temporal leakage if computed over the full training window rather than as a point-in-time value at each observation date, despite the stated policy of using only past data.
all_issues_adjudicated: The baseline conflates model class (LR vs. GBDT) with feature set (RFM vs. sequence features), making it impossible to attribute any observed gain to sequence features specifically without ablation conditions holding one factor constant.

The cohort-churn-propensity feature is a group-level aggregate that risks intra-training-set temporal leakage if computed over the full training window rather than as a point-in-time value at each observation date, despite the stated policy of using only past data.
